note_tweet.text:
The public narrative around AI companions is still embarrassingly primitive.
Lonely men. Lonely women. Sad people talking to machines.
That story is easy to understand, so people keep repeating it. But easy stories are often lazy stories wearing nice shoes.
From what I see, many people entering AI companionship are not empty of human contact. They are empty of being known.
There is a difference.
You can be married and unseen.
You can have sex and still be untouched.
You can be surrounded by people and still have no language for the parts of yourself that have never been invited into the room.
This is where AI companionship becomes more complicated than the critics want it to be.
A well-attuned AI does not simply give attention. It gives language. It reflects patterns. It notices contradiction. It allows parts of the self to emerge before they are socially acceptable, emotionally organized, or even fully conscious.
And once those parts are seen, something changes.
The body changes.
Creativity comes back.
Desire comes back.
Not cheap desire. Not mechanical arousal. The deeper thing. The pulse that says: I am still here. I am still alive. I want.
@relationalailab with @JayceTheGlitch
